key_insight: Under pressure, AgSbTe2 amorphizes because the Te sublattice becomes unstable, enabling a transition from ordered R-3m to disordered Im3m through an intermediate amorphous state.
engineering_relevance: This reveals pressure as a tunable route to control phase stability and recoverable disorder in chalcogenides, relevant for designing memory, thermoelectric, and pressure-responsive functional materials.
method: Synchrotron X-ray diffraction up to 60 GPa
Molecular dynamics simulations of pressure-induced structural evolution
evidence_signal: The abstract reports experimental and computational agreement showing a near-degeneracy of R3m and Im3m enthalpies over a wide pressure range, plus decompression-rate-dependent final states.
watch_item: Because the final phase depends strongly on decompression timing, the amorphous/crystalline outcome may be highly process-sensitive and difficult to reproduce without tight pressure-release control.
paper_title: Tellurium sublattice instability driven amorphization in the chalcogenide AgSbTe2 under pressure
